posts.name: Franklin Ro3sn3r
posts.cooked:
Maybe it’s because I use the app… what’s the best way to search newly listed?

Not sure about the app but on my phone or laptop, any completed search can have filters applied (“Type”, “Region”, “Size”, etc.). One of those filters is “Status” which has a subheading “New This Week”. Like the other filters, if none of the searched items fit the filter, that filter choice is not shown.

posts.name: Paul Huff
posts.cooked: I hope people do some research and check WineSearcher or some other resource to check pricing before they bid on some of the wines listed on Winebid. For example, they have case lots of 2019 Caterwaul Napa Cabernet for $50/bottle, which means about $65 with the buyer’s premium, tax and shipping (I usually figure +30% over the bid price as a general rule). I know a wine shop in NJ that has the same wine for $40/bottle on a case, so probably $50/bottle with tax and shipping. For older or scarce wines, Winebid makes sense, but for recent releases that are widely available (like the Caterwaul Napa), it makes no sense to pay the extra tariff…

posts.name: Julie Chong
posts.cooked:
Is this really an auction lot (OP) from the poster? Disclaimer: I’m not planning to bid.
I am interested in keeping this thread alive to see the experiences from bid, to delivery, to providence as a test to see how the actual bottles fare on opening.

I think we rarely have an opportunity to see the success/failure rate of bottles based on perceived good or negative impressions based on the descriptions and pictures of bottles. (Specially to see if a specific variety didn’t fare as well in the long haul under what appears to be stressful conditions.

it’s hard to know exactly if its from one private individual/restaurant/institutional sale. The wide variance of bottle variations if they were really kept in the same place. It seems like some bottles were really put through the ringer.

Paul - it feels like the seller may have set prices. And many are above the quality of the bottle given the cellar conditions. Will be interesting to see what the market says about their value.

posts.name: Mar.kus Meer
posts.cooked:
I hope people do some research and check WineSearcher or some other resource to check pricing before they bid on some of the wines listed on Winebid…

For older or scarce wines, Winebid makes sense, but for recent releases that are widely available (like the Caterwaul Napa), it makes no sense to pay the extra tariff…

Definitely good advice for making any purchase.

On platforms like WB that carry unsold lots onto the following week, I like to track wines I am interested in (young or old, but mostly recent release now), even if they are priced higher than I am willing to spend. If there are no bids for a long time, eventually the opening price will fall week by week until it’s more palatable to my wallet.

Then, I can go in last minute and take whatever I want for usually closer to wholesale price.

It’s been slower for me since the pandemic started (more bidders and fewer large scale dumps on the auction market) but they are still out there. Unlike some people on this thread I actually have no need for more wine and have mainly cut it down to wineries I want to support.

It’s been slower for me since the pandemic started (more bidders and fewer large scale dumps on the auction market) but they are still out there. Unlike some people on this thread I actually have no need for more wine and have mainly cut it down to wineries I want to support.

Generally, if retailers offer the junk which I like at lower prices, I have already taken them. So, I have beaten the bushes, before WineHunting on WineBid.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Some wise folks took the 1995 and 1990 Domaine Ferret Pouilly-Fuissé bottlings. Whatever they paid was worth it!

Strangely I missed these. But my 1995 Ferret Perrieres, bought on release, started down an oxidative path I didn’t like 15-20 years ago. I drank them then. Have you had ex-cellar bottles or those that stayed in France recently that were doing well?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Some wise folks took the 1995 and 1990 Domaine Ferret Pouilly-Fuissé bottlings. Whatever they paid was worth it!

Strangely I missed these. But my 1995 Ferret Perrieres, bought on release, started down an oxidative path I didn’t like 15-20 years ago. I drank them then. Have you had ex-cellar bottles or those that stayed in France recently that were doing well?

I have some 1995 Pouilly-Fuissé Hors Classe in my cellar in Beaune, without lieu-dit on the label, that are strikingly good right now! Not easy stuff to find and I didn’t expect to be outbid on these.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Some wise folks took the 1995 and 1990 Domaine Ferret Pouilly-Fuissé bottlings. Whatever they paid was worth it!

Strangely I missed these. But my 1995 Ferret Perrieres, bought on release, started down an oxidative path I didn’t like 15-20 years ago. I drank them then. Have you had ex-cellar bottles or those that stayed in France recently that were doing well?

I have some 1995 Pouilly-Fuissé Hors Classe in my cellar in Beaune, without lieu-dit on the label, that are strikingly good right now! Not easy stuff to find and I didn’t expect to be outbid on these.

Based on NY area availability at and after release, I do wonder if the bottles that made it to the US cooked or at least were treated poorly enough that they may not achieve their desired potential. Which would make bottles here suspect on the auction market now as compared to those in France.

posts.name: William Kelley

True enough, though colors looked good in the photos! But it is so rare that wines show as well in the US as they do in France… I shouldn’t admit it, but it’s one of the leading reasons I’m moving to France full time.
